critique.summary: The article claims Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle programs, citing strategic missteps and industry challenges, which could hinder its ability to compete in the evolving automotive market. It discusses the implications of this decision on Honda's technological development and market position.
mainPoints: Honda has halted development and production of several EV models, including the Acura RDX EV, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and the GM-designed Prologue.
The article argues that Honda's approach to EVs and software-defined vehicles is outdated, risking falling behind industry leaders like Tesla and BYD.
Honda's reliance on internal combustion engines and traditional car design may limit its ability to adapt to industry shifts towards electrification and software upgrades.
The article highlights that Honda's decision could lead to missed opportunities in learning, supply chain development, and customer feedback in the EV space.
It suggests Honda faces an identity crisis, struggling to redefine itself beyond its legacy internal combustion engine reputation.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a mix of factual information, such as Honda halting specific EV projects and industry trends, supported by external sources like Automotive News. However, some claims about Honda's strategic failures and future prospects are speculative and lack detailed evidence, making the overall assessment somewhat subjective. The discussion about industry shifts and Honda's position aligns with known market trends, but the portrayal of Honda's strategy as entirely flawed may be an oversimplification.
considerations: The article does not mention Honda's ongoing or future plans for EV development or partnerships beyond the halted projects.
It overlooks potential technological or market developments that could influence Honda's strategy or recovery.
The analysis does not include perspectives from Honda executives or industry analysts who might provide a more balanced view.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Honda's decision to shelve its EV plans, with opinions on the broader implications for the automotive industry, technology, and consumer preferences. Many comments express skepticism about EVs, software-defined vehicles, and industry trends, while some highlight regional differences and Honda's strategic choices.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-reasoned critique of Honda's approach to SDVs, emphasizing potential industry disruption and consumer dissatisfaction.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text:
"Here, Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time."

I'll pay triple for a non software defined vehicle that doesn't track me and can't be touched by the dealer once I purchase it. My one SDV (Tesla) is still on FSD from 2023 because the newer versions are terrible judging from the comments on the Tesla forums.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights consumer concerns about privacy and control over their vehicles, aligning with broader skepticism about SDVs.

comments.author: mullingitover
comments.text:
I'm convinced that the Japanese government is terrified of EVs because all the small and medium-sized businesses which support the Japanese auto industry will be absolutely gutted when vehicles contain drastically fewer parts.

That, and Japan is deeply screwed if they go all-in on EVs and then China decides they shouldn't be allowed access to any more rare earths.
comments.analysis: This comment offers an economic and political perspective on Japan's cautious stance towards EV adoption, though it is speculative.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
I think Japanese automakers by sticking to ICE vehicles have admitted defeat - that they no longer have the engineering prowess to compete.

they dominated in the era of small engines.

with EVs - the Chinese have run away with the stick & sadly no one is catching up.

I wish the Japanese made good EVs - Germans are the only ones besides the Chinese making decent EVs
comments.analysis: This comment presents a strong opinion on Japanese automakers' strategic shift, supported by industry trends but somewhat dismissive of their capabilities.

comments.author: pclowes
comments.text:
Everyone is saying EVs are the future but most EVs cannot compete with many of Honda’s offerings.

Eg. I need to move 6 people and significant gear (skiing, camping, biking etc) long remote distances.

There is no EV that can do that really. And the ones that come close are easily $20-30k higher than an Odyssey. Plus the durability of large EVs is far from proven while the 300k mile club of Odyssey owners is large.

I need Suburban/Minivan functionality out of a proven OEM at a competitive price point. (I also need to see my friends with Rivians etc not having to schedule their vacation around charger availability. Have seen this waste hours and hours of time)
comments.analysis: The comment highlights practical consumer needs that current EVs may not meet, emphasizing the continued relevance of traditional vehicles.

comments.author: troyvit
comments.text:
> By shelving EVs, Honda will fall farther behind in two of the biggest shifts sweeping the automotive industry: electric drivetrains [...]

Ugh that sucks

> [...] and software-defined vehicles.

Take my money! I'll suffer with gas for that.
comments.analysis: This comment sarcastically criticizes Honda's decision, prioritizing personal convenience over industry trends, but lacks nuanced analysis.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
This is so unfortunate. I was never a van guy, but my wife insisted we get a van, so I got the Honda. And honestly? I kinda love it. It drives like a car but holds eight people (or four people and a whole bunch of luggage).

The way we use the van, 90% of our drives are under 20 miles round trip. The rest are longer road trips. I've been waiting eight years for Honda to make an electric or even a plug-in hybrid where the gas motor just charge the battery.

It would be perfect for my family. I guess that's not happening now.
comments.analysis: Personal anecdote supporting Honda's utility, but less relevant to the industry-wide implications of EV shelving.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: A bold prediction about industry consolidation, reflecting concerns about the viability of traditional automakers in the EV era.

comments.author: proee
comments.text: Could it be that the EVs they were planning were just out of touch with what the market wants? Their zero vehicles look butt-ugly in my opinion. They look like concept cars that are great for show, but no serious buyer would consider them for a daily driver.
comments.analysis: Focuses on aesthetic opinions rather than substantive industry analysis, less relevant to the main discussion.

comments.author: speedgoose
comments.text:
I expected better from the company that entered the EV market with an impressive aquarium simulator in its Honda E.

Time will tell, but I think it’s a long term mistake.
comments.analysis: Expresses disappointment based on a specific model but lacks broader industry insight.
